summary: Joel shares two brief updates: his frustration with internet providers who can't even send email (preferring to fax quotes), and his excitement about a new wireless modem that lets him post from a cafe. The irony of an internet provider unable to use email drives his purchasing decision away from Qwest. He highlights SAVVIS as the likely winner for their office T1 line. The post captures a moment in early 2000s tech where wireless internet access from a cafe felt genuinely novel.
key_points: Internet providers soliciting T1 business couldn't manage to send a quote via email, insisting on fax instead
Joel uses a company's inability to use its own product category as a disqualifying criterion
Early wireless broadband (128kbps Ricochet modem) represented an exciting new frontier of mobile connectivity
money_quotes: Fax? Hello, people, you're Internet providers. Why not email me a quote?
I don't know why I would want to buy T1 access from an Internet company that doesn't know how to send an email.
key_insight: If a company can't competently use the very product it sells, that tells you everything you need to know about whether to buy from them.
